title: Security Policy
text: Please report security issues privately rather opening public issue. Preferred: open private report GitHub Security Advisories https://github.com/choxos/dagittyplus/security/advisories/new. Alternatively, email ahmad.pub@gmail.com. Include affected surface (R package, bundled Shiny app, web editor), description, steps reproduce. can expect acknowledgment within days.

title: Security Policy
text: Fixes applied latest release default branch. long-term support branch.

title: Security Policy
text: causal-analysis engine (inst/js/dagitty-alg.js, web app’s public/dagitty-alg.js) vendored build upstream DAGitty engine. Engine-level issues may also need reported upstream https://github.com/jtextor/dagitty. web editor bundled Shiny app run entirely client side transmit model data server.

title: A SEM user's guide to dagitty for R
text: Dagitty software analyze causal diagrams, also known directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). Structural equation models (SEMs) can viewed parametric form DAGs, encode linear functions instead arbitrary nonlinear functions. every SEM DAG, much methodology developed DAGs potentially great interest SEM users well. vignette, going show possibilities. follows structure Kyono’s “Commentator” program (http://ftp.cs.ucla.edu/pub/stat_ser/r364.pdf), thereby also shows tasks implemented program can solved using dagitty package.
code: g1 <- dagitty( "dag {     W1 -> Z1 -> X -> Y     Z1 <- V -> Z2     W2 -> Z2 -> Y     X <-> W1 <-> W2 <-> Y }")  g2 <- dagitty( "dag {     Y <- X <- Z1 <- V -> Z2 -> Y     Z1 <- W1 <-> W2 -> Z2     X <- W1 -> Y     X <- W2 -> Y }")  plot(graphLayout(g1))

what: List testable implications of a structural equation model
title: A SEM user's guide to dagitty for R
code: print( impliedConditionalIndependencies( g1 ) ) V _||_ W1 V _||_ W2 V _||_ X | W1, Z1 V _||_ Y | W1, W2, Z1, Z2 W1 _||_ Z2 | W2 W2 _||_ Z1 | W1 X _||_ Z2 | V, W2 X _||_ Z2 | W1, W2, Z1 Z1 _||_ Z2 | V, W2 Z1 _||_ Z2 | V, W1

what: List adjustment sets for specific path coefficients
title: A SEM user's guide to dagitty for R
code: print( adjustmentSets( g1, "Z1", "X", effect="direct" ) ) { W1 } print( adjustmentSets( g2, "X", "Y", effect="direct" ) ) { W1, W2, Z2 } { V, W1, W2 } { W1, W2, Z1 }

what: List path coefficients that are identifiable by regression
title: A SEM user's guide to dagitty for R
code: for( n in names(g1) ){     for( m in children(g1,n) ){         a <- adjustmentSets( g1, n, m, effect="direct" )         if( length(a) > 0 ){             cat("The coefficient on ",n,"->",m,                 " is identifiable controlling for:\n",sep="")             print( a, prefix=" * " )         }     } } The coefficient on V->Z1 is identifiable controlling for: {} The coefficient on V->Z2 is identifiable controlling for: {} The coefficient on W1->Z1 is identifiable controlling for: {} The coefficient on W2->Z2 is identifiable controlling for: {} The coefficient on Z1->X is identifiable controlling for: { W1 } The coefficient on Z2->Y is identifiable controlling for: { W1, W2, Z1 } { V, W2 }

what: List adjustment sets for specific total effects
title: A SEM user's guide to dagitty for R
code: print( adjustmentSets( g1, "X", "Y" ) ) print( adjustmentSets( g2, "X", "Y" ) ) { W1, W2, Z2 } { V, W1, W2 } { W1, W2, Z1 }

what: List total effects that are identifiable by regression
title: A SEM user's guide to dagitty for R
code: for( n in names(g1) ){     for( m in setdiff( descendants( g1, n ), n ) ){         a <- adjustmentSets( g1, n, m )         if( length(a) > 0 ){             cat("The total effect of ",n," on ",m,                 " is identifiable controlling for:\n",sep="")             print( a, prefix=" * " )         }     } } The total effect of V on Z2 is identifiable controlling for: {} The total effect of V on Y is identifiable controlling for: {} The total effect of V on Z1 is identifiable controlling for: {} The total effect of V on X is identifiable controlling for: {} The total effect of W1 on Z1 is identifiable controlling for: {} The total effect of W2 on Z2 is identifiable controlling for: {} The total effect of Z1 on X is identifiable controlling for: { W1 } The total effect of Z1 on Y is identifiable controlling for: { W1, W2, Z2 } { V, W1 } The total effect of Z2 on Y is identifiable controlling for: { W1, W2, Z1 } { V, W2 }

what: List path coefficients that are identifiable through instrumental variables
title: A SEM user's guide to dagitty for R
code: for( n in names(g1) ){     for( m in children(g1,n) ){         iv <- instrumentalVariables( g1, n, m )         if( length( iv ) > 0 ){             cat( n, m, "\n" )             print( iv , prefix=" * " )         }     } } V Z1   *  Z2 |  W1 V Z2   *  X |  W2  *  Z1 |  W2 W1 Z1   *  W2  *  Z2 |  V W2 Z2   *  W1  *  X |  V  *  Z1 |  V X Y   *  V |  W2, Z2  *  Z1 |  W1, W2, Z2 Z1 X   *  V  *  W2  *  Z2 Z2 Y   *  V |  X  *  W1 |  X  *  Z1 |  X

what: Graphical Analysis of Structural Causal Models
title: Graphical Analysis of Structural Causal Models
text: dagittyplus enhanced fork dagitty R package graphical analysis structural causal models, also known directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). computes covariate adjustment sets estimating causal effects, enumerates instrumental variables, derives testable implications (d-separation vanishing tetrads), generates equivalent models, simulates data model. upstream exported functions dagitty S3 class preserved, existing dagitty code ggdag package work unchanged. dagittyplus adds interfaces top: browser editor, bundled Shiny app via launch_dagittyplus(), selected-node helpers (selectedNodes() / selectedNodes<-).

title: Graphical Analysis of Structural Causal Models
text: redesigned, polished web editor runs entirely browser, R installation required: https://choxos.github.io/dagittyplus/app/

title: Graphical Analysis of Structural Causal Models
text: Install development version GitHub: package depends V8, runs bundled JavaScript analysis engine.
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("choxos/dagittyplus")

title: Graphical Analysis of Structural Causal Models
code: library(dagittyplus)  g <- dagitty("dag {   x [exposure]   y [outcome]   z -> x   z -> y   x -> y }")  # Which variables must we adjust for to estimate the total effect of x on y? adjustmentSets(g, exposure = "x", outcome = "y") #> { z }  # Testable implications of the model impliedConditionalIndependencies(g)  # Is the model acyclic? isAcyclic(g) #> [1] TRUE

title: Graphical Analysis of Structural Causal Models
text: dagittyplus returns standard dagitty objects, ggdag package can tidy plot ggplot2:
code: library(ggdag) ggdag(g)

title: Graphical Analysis of Structural Causal Models
text: package bundles point--click Shiny application. “Draw” tab embeds web editor, “Analyze” tab runs package’s functions model type, works offline:
code: # install.packages(c("shiny", "bslib")) library(dagittyplus) launch_dagittyplus()

title: Graphical Analysis of Structural Causal Models
text: dagittyplus builds dagitty engine. Please cite original papers: Textor, J., van der Zander, B., Gilthorpe, M. S., Liśkiewicz, M., & Ellison, G. T. H. (2016). Robust causal inference using directed acyclic graphs: R package ‘dagitty’. International Journal Epidemiology, 45(6), 1887–1894. https://doi.org/10.1093/ije/dyw341 Ankan, ., Wortel, . M. N., & Textor, J. (2021). Testing Graphical Causal Models Using R Package ‘dagitty’. Current Protocols, 1(2), e45. https://doi.org/10.1002/cpz1.45

title: Graphical Analysis of Structural Causal Models
text: GPL-2, license upstream dagitty package.

title: Ancestral Relations — AncestralRelations
text: Retrieve names variables given graph specified ancestral relationship input variable v.

title: Ancestral Relations — AncestralRelations
code: descendants(x, v, proper = FALSE)  ancestors(x, v, proper = FALSE)  children(x, v)  parents(x, v)  neighbours(x, v)  spouses(x, v)  adjacentNodes(x, v)  markovBlanket(x, v)

title: Ancestral Relations — AncestralRelations
text: x input graph, type. v name(s) variable(s). proper logical. default (proper=FALSE), descendants ancestors variable include variable . (proper=TRUE), variable included. descendants(x,v) retrieves variables reachable v via directed path. ancestors(x,v) retrieves variables v reachable via directed path. children(x,v) finds variables w connected v edge \(v\) -> \(w\). parents(x,v) finds variables w connected v edge \(w\) -> \(v\). markovBlanket(x,v) returns x's parents, children, parents children. Markov blanket always renders x independent nodes graph. convention, descendants(x,v) ancestors(x,v) include v children(x,v) parents(x,v) .

title: Ancestral Relations — AncestralRelations
code: g <- dagitty("graph{ a <-> x -> b ; c -- x <- d }") # Includes "x" descendants(g,"x") #> [1] "x" "b" # Does not include "x" descendants(g,"x",TRUE) #> [1] "b" parents(g,"x") #> [1] "d" spouses(g,"x")  #> [1] "a"

title: Generating Equivalent Models — EquivalentModels
text: equivalenceClass(x) generates complete partially directed acyclic graph (CPDAG) input DAG x. CPDAG represents graphs Markov equivalent x: undirected edges CPDAG can oriented either way, long create cycle new v-structure (sugraph -> m <- b, b adjacent).

title: Generating Equivalent Models — EquivalentModels
code: equivalenceClass(x)  equivalentDAGs(x, n = 100)

title: Generating Equivalent Models — EquivalentModels
text: x input graph, DAG (CPDAG equivalentDAGs). n maximal number returned graphs.

title: Generating Equivalent Models — EquivalentModels
text: equivalentDAGs(x,n) enumerates n DAGs Markov equivalent input DAG CPDAG x.

title: Generating Equivalent Models — EquivalentModels
code: # How many equivalent DAGs are there for the sports DAG example? g <- getExample("Shrier") length(equivalentDAGs(g)) #> [1] 6 # Plot all equivalent DAGs par( mfrow=c(2,3) ) lapply( equivalentDAGs(g), plot )  #> [[1]] #> [[1]]$mar #> [1] 0 0 0 0 #>  #>  #> [[2]] #> [[2]]$mar #> [1] 0 0 0 0 #>  #>  #> [[3]] #> [[3]]$mar #> [1] 0 0 0 0 #>  #>  #> [[4]] #> [[4]]$mar #> [1] 0 0 0 0 #>  #>  #> [[5]] #> [[5]]$mar #> [1] 0 0 0 0 #>  #>  #> [[6]] #> [[6]]$mar #> [1] 0 0 0 0 #>  #>  # How many edges can be reversed without changing the equivalence class? sum(edges(equivalenceClass(g))$e == "--") #> [1] 3

title: Variable Statuses — VariableStatus
text: Get set variables given status graph. Variables dagitty graphs can one several statuses. Variables status exposure outcome important determining causal effects via functions adjustmentSets instrumentalVariables. Variables status latent assumed unobserved variables latent constructs, respected deriving testable implications graph via functions impliedConditionalIndependencies vanishingTetrads.

title: Variable Statuses — VariableStatus
code: exposures(x)  exposures(x) <- value  outcomes(x)  outcomes(x) <- value  latents(x)  latents(x) <- value  adjustedNodes(x)  adjustedNodes(x) <- value  selectedNodes(x)  selectedNodes(x) <- value  setVariableStatus(x, status, value)

title: Variable Statuses — VariableStatus
text: x input graph, type. value character vector; names variables receive given status. status character, one "exposure", "outcome", "latent", "adjustedNode", "selected".

title: Variable Statuses — VariableStatus
text: setVariableStatus first removes given status variables graph , sets given variables. instance,  status="exposure"  value="X" given, X exposure resulting graph.

title: Variable Statuses — VariableStatus
code: g <- dagitty("dag{ x<->m<->y<-x }") # m-bias graph exposures(g) <- "x" outcomes(g) <- "y" adjustmentSets(g) #> {}

title: Covariate Adjustment Sets — adjustmentSets
text: Enumerates sets covariates (asymptotically) allow unbiased estimation causal effects observational data, assuming input causal graph correct.

title: Covariate Adjustment Sets — adjustmentSets
code: adjustmentSets(   x,   exposure = NULL,   outcome = NULL,   type = c("minimal", "canonical", "all"),   effect = c("total", "direct"),   max.results = Inf )

title: Covariate Adjustment Sets — adjustmentSets
text: x input graph, DAG, MAG, PDAG, PAG. exposure name(s) exposure variable(s). given (default), exposure variables supposed defined graph . outcome name(s) outcome variable(s), also taken graph given. type type adjustment set(s) compute. type="minimal", minimal sufficient adjustment sets returned (default). type="", valid adjustment sets returned. type="canonical", single adjustment set returned consists (possible) ancestors exposures outcomes, minus (possible) descendants nodes proper causal paths. canonical adjustment set always valid valid set exists . effect effect identified. effect="total", total effect identified, adjustment criterion Perkovic et al (2015; see also van der Zander et al., 2014), extension Pearl's back-door criterion, used. Otherwise, effect="direct", average direct effect identified, Pearl's single-door criterion used (Pearl, 2009). structural equation model (Gaussian graphical model), direct effects simply path coefficients. max.results integer. listing adjustment set stopped many results found. Use Inf generate . applys type="minimal".

title: Covariate Adjustment Sets — adjustmentSets
text: input graph MAG PAG, must contain undirected edges (=hidden selection variables).

title: Covariate Adjustment Sets — adjustmentSets
text: J. Pearl (2009), Causality: Models, Reasoning Inference. Cambridge University Press. B. van der Zander, M. Liskiewicz J. Textor (2014), Constructing separators adjustment sets ancestral graphs. Proceedings UAI 2014. E. Perkovic, J. Textor, M. Kalisch M. H. Maathuis (2015), Complete Generalized Adjustment Criterion. Proceedings UAI 2015.

title: Covariate Adjustment Sets — adjustmentSets
code: # The M-bias graph showing that adjustment for  # pre-treatment covariates is not always valid g <- dagitty( "dag{ x -> y ; x <-> m <-> y }" ) adjustmentSets( g, "x", "y" ) # empty set #> {} # Generate data where true effect (=path coefficient) is .5 set.seed( 123 ); d <- simulateSEM( g, .5, .5 ) confint( lm( y ~ x, d ) )["x",] # includes .5 #>     2.5 %    97.5 %  #> 0.3888421 0.5543539  confint( lm( y ~ x + m, d ) )["x",] # does not include .5 #>      2.5 %     97.5 %  #> 0.08594607 0.22524480   # Adjustment sets can also sometimes be computed for graphs in which not all  # edge directions are known g <- dagitty("pdag { x[e] y[o] a -- {i z b}; {a z i} -> x -> y <- {z b} }") adjustmentSets( g ) #> { b, z } #> { a, z }

title: Ancestor Graph — ancestorGraph
text: Creates induced subgraph containing vertices v, ancestors, edges . vertices edges discarded.

title: Ancestor Graph — ancestorGraph
code: ancestorGraph(x, v = NULL)

title: Ancestor Graph — ancestorGraph
text: x input graph, DAG, MAG, PDAG. v variable names.

title: Ancestor Graph — ancestorGraph
text: input graph MAG PDAG, *possible* ancestors returned (see Examples).

title: Ancestor Graph — ancestorGraph
code: g <- dagitty("dag{ z <- x -> y }") ancestorGraph( g, "z" ) #> dag { #> x #> z #> x -> z #> }  g <- dagitty("pdag{ z -- x -> y }") ancestorGraph( g, "y" ) # includes z #> pdag { #> x #> y #> z #> x -- z #> x -> y #> }

title: Convert to DAGitty object — as.dagitty

title: Convert to DAGitty object — as.dagitty
text: x object. ... arguments passed methods.

title: Back-Door Graph — backDoorGraph
text: Removes every first edge proper causal path x. x MAG PAG, “visible” directed edges removed (Zhang, 2008).

title: Back-Door Graph — backDoorGraph
code: backDoorGraph(x)

title: Back-Door Graph — backDoorGraph
text: x input graph, DAG, MAG, PDAG, PAG.

title: Back-Door Graph — backDoorGraph
text: J. Zhang (2008), Causal Reasoning Ancestral Graphs. Journal Machine Learning Research 9: 1437-1474.

title: Back-Door Graph — backDoorGraph
code: g <- dagitty( "dag { x <-> m <-> y <- x }" ) backDoorGraph( g ) # x->y edge is removed #> dag { #> m #> x #> y #> m <-> x #> m <-> y #> x -> y #> }  g <- dagitty( "mag { x <-> m <-> y <- x }" ) backDoorGraph( g ) # x->y edge is not removed #> mag { #> m #> x #> y #> m <-> x #> m <-> y #> x -> y #> }  g <- dagitty( "mag { x <-> m <-> y <- x <- i }" ) backDoorGraph( g ) # x->y edge is removed #> mag { #> i #> m #> x #> y #> i -> x #> m <-> x #> m <-> y #> x -> y #> }

title: Canonicalize an Ancestral Graph — canonicalize
text: Takes input ancestral graph (graph directed, bidirected undirected edges) converts DAG replacing every bidirected edge x <-> y substructure x <- L -> y, L latent variable, every undirected edge x – y substructure x -> S <- y, S selection variable. function check whether input actually ancestral graph.

title: Canonicalize an Ancestral Graph — canonicalize
code: canonicalize(x)

title: Canonicalize an Ancestral Graph — canonicalize
text: x input graph, DAG MAG.

title: Canonicalize an Ancestral Graph — canonicalize
text: list containing following components: g resulting graph. L Names newly inserted latent variables. S Names newly inserted selection variables.

title: Canonicalize an Ancestral Graph — canonicalize
code: canonicalize("mag{x<->y--z}") # introduces two new variables #> $g #> dag { #> L1 [latent] #> S1 [selected] #> x #> y #> z #> L1 -> x #> L1 -> y #> y -> S1 #> z -> S1 #> } #>  #> $L #> [1] "L1" #>  #> $S #> [1] "S1" #>

title: Generate Complete DAG — completeDAG
text: Generates complete DAG given variable names. order variables given corresponds topological ordering DAG. Returns named list.

title: Generate Complete DAG — completeDAG
code: completeDAG(x)

title: Generate Complete DAG — completeDAG
text: x variable names. Can also positive integer, case variables called  x1,...,xN.

code: convert(x, to, ...)

what: Plot Coordinates of Variables in Graph — coordinates
title: Plot Coordinates of Variables in Graph — coordinates
text: DAGitty syntax allows specification plot coordinates variable graph. function extracts plot coordinates graph description dagitty object. Note coordinate system undefined, typically one needs compute bounding box plotting graph.

title: Plot Coordinates of Variables in Graph — coordinates
code: coordinates(x)  coordinates(x) <- value

title: Plot Coordinates of Variables in Graph — coordinates
text: x input graph, type. value list components x y, giving relative coordinates variable. format suitable xy.coords.

title: Plot Coordinates of Variables in Graph — coordinates
code: ## Plot localization of each node in the Shrier example plot( coordinates( getExample("Shrier") ) )   ## Define a graph and set coordinates afterwards x <- dagitty('dag{     G <-> H <-> I <-> G     D <- B -> C -> I <- F <- B <- A     H <- E <- C -> G <- D }') coordinates( x ) <-     list( x=c(A=1, B=2, D=3, C=3, F=3, E=4, G=5, H=5, I=5),         y=c(A=0, B=0, D=1, C=0, F=-1, E=0, G=1, H=0, I=-1) ) plot( x )

title: Parse DAGitty Graph — dagitty
text: Constructs dagitty graph object textual description.

title: Parse DAGitty Graph — dagitty
code: dagitty(x, layout = FALSE)

title: Parse DAGitty Graph — dagitty
text: x character, string describing graphical model dagitty syntax. layout logical, whether automatically generate layout coordinates variable (see graphLayout)

title: Parse DAGitty Graph — dagitty
text: textual syntax DAGitty graph based dot language graphviz software (https://graphviz.gitlab.io/_pages/doc/info/lang.html). fairly intuitive syntax – use examples functions get started. important difference graphviz DAGitty language supports several types graphs, different semantics. However, many users mainly focus DAGs. DAGitty graph description following form: [graph type] '{' [statements] '}' [graph type] one 'dag', 'mag', 'pdag', 'pag' [statements] list variables statements edge statements, may (optionally) separated semicolons. Whitespace, including newlines, semantic role. Variable statments look like [variable id] '[' [properties] ']' example, statement x [exposure,pos="1,0"] declares variable ID x exposure variable layout position 1,0. edge statement x -> y declares directed edge variable x variable y. Explicit variable statements required variables involved edge statements, unless attributes position exposure/outcome status need set. DAGs (directed acyclic graphs) can contain following edges: ->, <->. Bidirected edges DAGs simply shorthands substructures <- U ->, U unobserved variable. MAGs (maximal ancestral graphs) can contain following edges: ->, <->, --. bidirected directed edges MAGs can represent latent confounders, undirected edges represent latent selection variables. details, see Richardson Spirtes (2002). PDAGs (partially directed acyclic graphs) can contain following edges: ->, <->, --. bidirected edges mean thing DAGs. undirected edges represent edges whose direction known. Thus, PDAGs used represent equivalence classes DAGs (see also function equivalenceClass). PAGs (partial ancestral graphs) MAGs PDAGs DAGs: represent equivalence classes MAGs. MAGs can contain following edges: @-@, ->, @->, --, @-- (@ symbols written circle marks literature). details PAGs, see Zhang et al (2008). now, DAGitty functions support PAGs (instance, adjustmentSets. DAGitty parser perform semantic validation. , check whether DAG actually acyclic, whether chain components PAG actually chordal. done can computationally rather expensive.

title: Parse DAGitty Graph — dagitty
text: Richardson, Thomas; Spirtes, Peter (2002), Ancestral graph Markov models. Annals Statistics 30(4): 962-1030. J. Zhang (2008), Causal Reasoning Ancestral Graphs. Journal Machine Learning Research 9: 1437-1474. B. van der Zander M. Liskiewicz (2016), Separators Adjustment Sets Markov Equivalent DAGs. Proceedings Thirtieth AAAI Conference Artificial Intelligence (AAAI'16), Phoenix, Arizona, USA.

title: Parse DAGitty Graph — dagitty
code: # Specify a simple DAG containing one path g <- dagitty("dag{    a -> b ;   b -> c ;   d -> c  }") # Newlines and semicolons are optional g <- dagitty("dag{    a -> b b -> c c -> d  }") # Paths can be specified in one go; the semicolon below is # optional g <- dagitty("dag{    a -> b ->c ; c -> d  }") # Edges can be written in reverse notation g <- dagitty("dag{    a -> b -> c <- d  }") # Spaces are optional as well g <- dagitty("dag{a->b->c<-d}") # Variable attributes can be set in square brackets # Example: DAG with one exposure, one outcome, and one unobserved variable g <- dagitty("dag{   x -> y ; x <- z -> y   x [exposure]   y [outcome]   z [unobserved] }")  # The same graph as above g <- dagitty("dag{x[e]y[o]z[u]x<-z->y<-x}") # A two-factor latent variable model g <- dagitty("dag {   X <-> Y   X -> a X -> b X -> c X -> d   Y -> a Y -> b Y -> c Y -> d }") # Curly braces can be used to "group" variables and  # specify edges to whole groups of variables # The same two-factor model g <- dagitty("dag{ {X<->Y} -> {a b c d} }") # A MAG g <- dagitty("mag{ a -- x -> y <-> z }") # A PDAG g <- dagitty("pdag{ x -- y -- z }") # A PAG g <- dagitty("pag{ x @-@ y @-@ z }")

title: d-Separation — dconnected
text: set Z d-separates path p (1) Z contains non-collider p, e.g. x->m->y Z=c("m"); (2) collider p Z, e.g. x->m<-y Z=c().

title: d-Separation — dconnected
code: dconnected(x, X, Y = list(), Z = list())  dseparated(x, X, Y = list(), Z = list())

title: d-Separation — dconnected
text: x input graph, DAG, PDAG, MAG. X vector variable names. Y vector variable names. Z vector variable names. dseparated(x,X,Y,Z) checks paths X Y d-separated Z. dconnected(x,X,Y,Z) checks least one path X Y d-separated Z.

title: d-Separation — dconnected
text: functions also work mixed graphs directed, undirected, bidirected edges. definition collider graphs : node two arrowheads collide, e.g. x<->m<-y x->m–y.

title: d-Separation — dconnected
code: dconnected( "dag{x->m->y}", "x", "y", c() ) # TRUE #> [1] TRUE dconnected( "dag{x->m->y}", "x", "y", c("m") ) # FALSE #> [1] FALSE dseparated( "dag{x->m->y}", "x", "y", c() ) # FALSE  #> [1] FALSE dseparated( "dag{x->m->y}", "x", "y", c("m") ) # TRUE #> [1] TRUE

title: Load Graph from dagitty.net — downloadGraph
text: x dagitty model URL.

title: Graph Edges — edges
text: data frame following variables: v name start node. w name end node. symmetric edges (bidirected undirected),  order start end node arbitrary. e type edge. Can one "->", "<->" "--". x X coordinate control point. NA, edge  drawn xspline start point, control point,  end point. especially important cases  one edge two variables (instance, directed bidirected edge). y Y coordinate control point.

title: Graph Edges — edges
code: ## Which kinds of edges are used in the Shrier example? levels( edges( getExample("Shrier") )$e ) #> NULL

title: Get Bundled Examples — getExample
text: Provides access builtin examples dagitty website.

title: Get Bundled Examples — getExample
code: getExample(x)

title: Get Bundled Examples — getExample
text: x name example, part thereof. Supported values : "M-bias" M-bias graph. "confounding" extended confounding triangle. "mediator" small model mediator. "paths" graph many variables paths "Sebastiani" small part genetics study (Sebastiani et al., 2005) "Polzer" DAG dentistry study (Polzer et al., 2012) "Schipf" DAG study diabetes (Schipf et al., 2010) "Shrier" DAG classic sports medicine example (Shrier & Platt, 2008) "Thoemmes" DAG unobserved variables (communicated Felix Thoemmes, 2013) "Kampen" DAG psychiatry study (van Kampen, 2014)

title: Get Bundled Examples — getExample
text: Sabine Schipf, Robin Haring, Nele Friedrich, Matthias Nauck, Katharina Lau, Dietrich Alte, Andreas Stang, Henry Voelzke, Henri Wallaschofski (2011), Low total testosterone associated increased risk incident type 2 diabetes mellitus men: Results study health pomerania (SHIP). Aging Male 14(3):168–75. Paola Sebastiani, Marco F. Ramoni, Vikki Nolan, Clinton T. Baldwin, Martin H. Steinberg (2005), Genetic dissection prognostic modeling overt stroke sickle cell anemia. Nature Genetics, 37:435–440. Ian Shrier Robert W. Platt (2008), Reducing bias directed acyclic graphs. BMC Medical Research Methodology, 8(70). Ines Polzer, Christian Schwahn, Henry Voelzke, Torsten Mundt, Reiner Biffar (2012), association tooth loss -cause circulatory  mortality. benefit replaced teeth? systematic review  meta-analysis. Clinical Oral Investigations, 16(2):333–351. Dirk van Kampen (2014), SSQ model schizophrenic prodromal unfolding revised:  analysis causal chains based language directed graphs. European Psychiatry, 29(7):437–48.

title: Get Bundled Examples — getExample
code: g <- getExample("Shrier") plot(g)

title: Generate Graph Layout — graphLayout
text: function generates plot coordinates variable graph already. end, well-known “Spring” layout algorithm used. Note stochastic algorithm, generated layout different every time (also means can try several times find decent layout).

title: Generate Graph Layout — graphLayout
code: graphLayout(x, method = "spring")

title: Generate Graph Layout — graphLayout
text: x input graph, type. method layout method; currently, "spring" supported.

title: Generate Graph Layout — graphLayout
text: graph x layout coordinates added.

title: Generate Graph Layout — graphLayout
code: ## Generate a layout for the M-bias graph and plot it plot( graphLayout( dagitty( "dag { X <- U1 -> M <- U2 -> Y } " ) ) )  ## Plot larger graph and abbreviate its variable names. plot( getExample("Shrier"), abbreviate.names=TRUE )

title: List Implied Conditional Independencies — impliedConditionalIndependencies
text: Generates list conditional independence statements must hold every probability distribution compatible given model.

title: List Implied Conditional Independencies — impliedConditionalIndependencies
code: impliedConditionalIndependencies(x, type = "missing.edge", max.results = Inf)

title: List Implied Conditional Independencies — impliedConditionalIndependencies
text: x input graph, DAG, MAG, PDAG. type can one "missing.edge", "basis.set", ".pairs". first, one minimal testable implication (smallest possible conditioning set) returned per missing edge graph. "basis.set", one testable implication returned per vertex graph non-descendants parents. Basis sets can smaller, involve higher-dimensional independencies, whereas missing edge sets involve independencies two variables time. ".pairs", function return list implied conditional independencies two variables time. Beware, can long list may feasible compute except small graphs. max.results integer. listing conditional independencies stopped many results found. Use Inf generate . applies type="missing.edge" type="".

title: List Implied Conditional Independencies — impliedConditionalIndependencies
code: g <- dagitty( "dag{ x -> m -> y }" ) impliedConditionalIndependencies( g ) # one #> x _||_ y | m latents( g ) <- c("m") impliedConditionalIndependencies( g ) # none

title: Implied Covariance Matrix of a Gaussian Graphical Model — impliedCovarianceMatrix
text: x input graph, DAG (may contain bidirected edges). b.default default path coefficient applied arrows coefficient defined model syntax. b.lower lower bound random path coefficients, applied b.default=NULL. b.upper upper bound path coefficients. eps residual variance (meaningful standardized=FALSE). standardized logical. true, standardized population covariance matrix generated (variables variance 1).

title: Find Instrumental Variables — instrumentalVariables
text: Generates list instrumental variables can used infer total effect exposure outcome presence latent confounding, linearity assumptions.

code: instrumentalVariables(x, exposure = NULL, outcome = NULL)

title: Find Instrumental Variables — instrumentalVariables
text: x input graph, DAG. exposure name exposure variable. given (default), exposure variable supposed defined graph . single exposure variable single outcome variable supported. outcome name outcome variable, also taken graph given. single outcome variable supported.

title: Find Instrumental Variables — instrumentalVariables
text: B. van der Zander, J. Textor M. Liskiewicz (2015), Efficiently Finding Conditional Instruments Causal Inference. Proceedings 24th International Joint Conference Artificial Intelligence (IJCAI 2015), pp. 3243-3249. AAAI Press, 2015.

title: Find Instrumental Variables — instrumentalVariables
code: # The classic IV model instrumentalVariables( "dag{ i->x->y; x<->y }", "x", "y" ) #>  i # A conditional instrumental variable instrumentalVariables( "dag{ i->x->y; x<->y ; y<-z->i }", "x", "y" ) #>  i |  z

title: Test for Cycles — isAcyclic
code: g1 <- dagitty("dag{X -> Y -> Z}") stopifnot( isTRUE(isAcyclic( g1 )) ) g2 <- dagitty("dag{X -> Y -> Z -> X}") stopifnot( isTRUE(!isAcyclic( g2 )) ) g3 <- dagitty("mag{X -- Y -- Z -- X}") stopifnot( isTRUE(isAcyclic( g3 )) )

title: Adjustment Criterion — isAdjustmentSet
text: Test whether set fulfills adjustment criterion, means, removes confounding bias estimating *total* effect. #' Back-door criterion (Shpitser et al, 2010; van der Zander et al, 2014; Perkovic et al, 2015) complete sense either set fulfills criterion, remove confounding bias.

title: Adjustment Criterion — isAdjustmentSet
text: x input graph, DAG, MAG, PDAG, PAG. Z vector variable names. exposure name(s) exposure variable(s). given (default), exposure variables supposed defined graph . outcome name(s) outcome variable(s), also taken graph given.

title: Adjustment Criterion — isAdjustmentSet
text: input graph MAG PAG, must contain undirected edges (=hidden selection variables).

title: Adjustment Criterion — isAdjustmentSet
text: E. Perkovic, J. Textor, M. Kalisch M. H. Maathuis (2015), Complete Generalized Adjustment Criterion. Proceedings UAI 2015. . Shpitser, T. VanderWeele J. M. Robins (2010), validity covariate adjustment estimating causal effects. Proceedings UAI 2010.

what: Convert Lavaan Model to DAGitty Graph — lavaanToGraph
title: Convert Lavaan Model to DAGitty Graph — lavaanToGraph
text: lavaan package popular package structural equation modeling. provide interoperability lavaan, function converts models specified lavaan syntax dagitty graphs.

title: Convert Lavaan Model to DAGitty Graph — lavaanToGraph
code: lavaanToGraph(x, digits = 3, ...)

title: Convert Lavaan Model to DAGitty Graph — lavaanToGraph
text: x data frame, lavaan parameter table returned lavaanify. Can also lavaan object lavaan model string. digits number significant digits use representing path coefficients, ... used.

title: Convert Lavaan Model to DAGitty Graph — lavaanToGraph
code:
if( require(lavaan) ){ mdl <- lavaanify(" X ~ C1 + C3 M ~ X + C3 Y ~ X + M + C3 + C5 C1 ~ C2 C3 ~ C2 + C4 C5 ~ C4 C1 ~~ C2 
 C1 ~~ C3 
 C1 ~~ C4 
 C1 ~~ C5 C2 ~~ C3 
 C2 ~~ C4 
 C2 ~~ C5 C3 ~~ C4 
 C3 ~~ C5",fixed.x=FALSE) plot( lavaanToGraph( mdl ) ) } #> Loading required package: lavaan #> This is lavaan 0.6-21 #> lavaan is FREE software! Please report any bugs. #> Plot coordinates for graph not supplied! Generating coordinates, see ?coordinates for how to set your own.

title: Test Graph against Data — localTests
text: Derives testable implications given graphical model tests given dataset.

title: Test Graph against Data — localTests
code: localTests(   x = NULL,   data = NULL,   type = c("cis", "cis.loess", "cis.chisq", "cis.pillai", "tetrads", "tetrads.within",     "tetrads.between", "tetrads.epistemic"),   tests = NULL,   sample.cov = NULL,   sample.nobs = NULL,   conf.level = 0.95,   R = NULL,   max.conditioning.variables = NULL,   abbreviate.names = TRUE,   tol = NULL,   loess.pars = NULL )  ciTest(X, Y, Z = NULL, data, ...)

title: Test Graph against Data — localTests
text: x input graph, DAG, MAG, PDAG. Either input graph explicit list tests needs specified. data matrix data frame containing data. type character indicating kind local test perform. Supported values "cis" (linear conditional independence), "cis.loess" (conditional independence using loess regression), "cis.chisq" (categorical data, based chi-square test), "cis.pillai" (mixed data, based canonical correlations), "tetrads" "tetrads.type", "type" one items tetrad typology, e.g. "tetrads.within" (see vanishingTetrads). Tetrad testing implemented DAGs. tests list precise tests perform. given, list tests automatically derived input graph. Can used restrict testing certain subset tests (instance, test conditional independencies conditioning set reasonably low dimension, shown example). sample.cov sample covariance matrix; ignored data supplied. Either data sample.cov sample.nobs must supplied. sample.nobs number observations; ignored data supplied. conf.level determines size confidence intervals test statistics. R many bootstrap replicates estimating confidence intervals. NULL, confidence intervals based normal approximation. tetrads, normal approximation valid large samples even data normally distributed. max.conditioning.variables conditional independence testing,  parameter can used perform tests number conditioning variables exceed given value. High-dimensional conditional independence tests can unreliable. abbreviate.names logical. Whether abbreviate variable names (used row names returned data frame). tol bound value tolerated deviation local test value. default, perform two-sided test hypothesis theta=0. parameter given, test changes abs(theta)=tol versus abs(theta)>tol. loess.pars list parameter passed  loess   (type="cis.loess"), example smoothing range. ciTest(X,Y,Z,data) convenience function test single conditional independence independently DAG. X vector variable names. Y vector variable names. Z vector variable names. ... parameters passed ciTest localTests

title: Test Graph against Data — localTests
text: Tetrad implications can derived Gaussian model (.e., linear structural equation model) postulated. Conditional independence implications (CI) require assumption. However, Tetrad CI implications tested parametrically: Tetrads, Wishart's confidence interval formula used, whereas CIs, Z test zero conditional covariance (covariance matrix given) test residual independence linear regression (raw data given) performed. tetrad CI tests also support bootstrapping instead estimating parametric confidence intervals. canonical correlations approach, ordinal variables integer-coded, categorical variables dummy-coded (omitting dummy representing frequent category). text X _||_ Y | Z, first regress X Y (now can multivariate) Z, compute canonical correlations residuals. effect size root mean square canonical correlation (closely related Pillai's trace, root squared sum canonical correlations).

title: Test Graph against Data — localTests
code: # Simulate full mediation model with measurement error of M1 set.seed(123) d <- simulateSEM("dag{X->{U1 M2}->Y U1->M1}",.6,.6)  # Postulate and test full mediation model without measurement error r <- localTests( "dag{ X -> {M1 M2} -> Y }", d, "cis" ) plotLocalTestResults( r )   # Simulate data from example SEM g <- getExample("Polzer") d <- simulateSEM(g,.1,.1)  # Compute independencies with at most 3 conditioning variables r <- localTests( g, d, "cis.loess", R=100, loess.pars=list(span=0.6),         max.conditioning.variables=3 ) plotLocalTestResults( r )   # Test independencies for categorical data using chi-square test d <- simulateLogistic("dag{X->{U1 M2}->Y U1->M1}",2) localTests( "dag{X->{M1 M2}->Y}", d, type="cis.chisq" ) #>                        rmsea       x2 df    p.value rmsea 2.5% rmsea 97.5% #> M1 _||_ M2 | X    0.10154268 8.266720  2 0.01602893          0   0.2382388 #> X _||_ Y | M1, M2 0.09931406 6.576621  3 0.08668925          0   0.3220601

what: Simulate Data from Structural Equation Model — simulateSEM
title: Simulate Data from Structural Equation Model — simulateSEM
text: Interprets input graph structural equation model, generates random path coefficients, simulates data model. bare-bones function probably useful except quick validation purposes (e.g. checking implied vanishing tetrad truly vanishes simulated data). elaborate simulation studies, please use lavaan package similar facilities packages.

title: Simulate Data from Structural Equation Model — simulateSEM
code: simulateSEM(   x,   b.default = NULL,   b.lower = -0.6,   b.upper = 0.6,   eps = 1,   N = 500,   standardized = TRUE,   empirical = FALSE,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Simulate Data from Structural Equation Model — simulateSEM
text: x input graph, DAG (may contain bidirected edges). b.default default path coefficient applied arrows coefficient defined model syntax. b.lower lower bound random path coefficients, applied b.default=NULL. b.upper upper bound path coefficients. eps residual variance (meaningful standardized=FALSE). N number samples generate. standardized logical. true, standardized population covariance matrix generated (variables variance 1). empirical logical. true, empirical covariance matrix equal population covariance matrix. verbose logical. true, prints generated population covariance matrix.

title: Simulate Data from Structural Equation Model — simulateSEM
text: Returns data frame containing N values variable x.

title: Simulate Data from Structural Equation Model — simulateSEM
text: Data generated following manner. directed arrow assigned path coefficient can given using attribute "beta" model syntax (see examples). coefficients set manner set b.default argument, given, chosen uniformly random interval given b.lower b.upper (inclusive; set parameters value constant path coefficients). bidirected arrow <-> b replaced substructure  <- L -> b, L exogenous latent variable. Path coefficients substructures set sqrt(x), x chosen random given interval; x negative, one path coefficient set -sqrt(x) sqrt(x). residual variances set eps. standardized=TRUE, path coefficients interpreted standardized coefficients. standardized coefficients compatible graph structures. instance, graph structure z <- x -> y -> z incompatible standardized coefficients 0.9, since imply variance z must larger 1. large graphs many parallel paths, can difficult find coefficients work.

title: Simulate Data from Structural Equation Model — simulateSEM
code: ## Simulate data with pre-defined path coefficients of -.6 g <- dagitty('dag{z -> x [beta=-.6] x <- y [beta=-.6] }') x <- simulateSEM( g )  cov(x) #>            x           y           z #> x  1.0411816 -0.60967927 -0.63667828 #> y -0.6096793  1.01562557  0.01269518 #> z -0.6366783  0.01269518  1.01392535

title: List Implied Vanishing Tetrads — vanishingTetrads
text: Interpret given graph structural equation model list vanishing tetrads implies.

title: List Implied Vanishing Tetrads — vanishingTetrads
code: vanishingTetrads(x, type = NA)

title: List Implied Vanishing Tetrads — vanishingTetrads
text: x input graph, DAG. type restrict output one level Kenny's tetrad typology. Possible values "within" (homogeneity within constructs; four variables parents), "" (homogeneity constructs; two pairs variables sharing one parent) "epistemic" (consistency epistemic correlations; three variables parent). default, tetrads listed.

title: List Implied Vanishing Tetrads — vanishingTetrads
text: data frame four columns, row form ,j,k,l means tetrad Cov(,j)Cov(k,l) - Cov(,k)Cov(j,l) vanishes (equal 0) according model.

title: List Implied Vanishing Tetrads — vanishingTetrads
text: Kenny, D. . (1979), Correlation Causality. Wiley, New York.

title: List Implied Vanishing Tetrads — vanishingTetrads
code: # Specify two-factor model with 4 indicators each g <- dagitty("dag{{x1 x2 x3 x4} <- x <-> y -> {y1 y2 y3 y4}}") latents(g) <- c("x","y")  # Check how many tetrads are implied nrow(vanishingTetrads(g)) #> [1] 138 # Check how these distribute across the typology nrow(vanishingTetrads(g,"within")) #> [1] 6 nrow(vanishingTetrads(g,"between")) #> [1] 36 nrow(vanishingTetrads(g,"epistemic")) #> [1] 96

title: dagittyplus 0.4.0
text: dagittyplus enhanced fork dagitty R package Johannes Textor colleagues. first release re-homes package modernizes tooling, without changing analysis behavior. Renamed package dagittyplus moved repository root installs remotes::install_github("choxos/dagittyplus") builds pkgdown site cleanly. Every exported function name dagitty S3 class preserved, existing dagitty code ggdag package keep working unchanged. Added pkgdown documentation website, Air code formatting, code coverage configuration. causal-analysis engine, redesigned web interface, bundled Shiny application driven JavaScript library powers R package, results stay consistent across every interface.  Added vignette, “Plotting dagittyplus models ggdag”, documenting preserved dagitty S3 class lets ggdag package plot dagittyplus models directly. Added cross-surface CI: engine-parity check (vendored DAGitty engine byte-identical across R package, bundled Shiny editor, web app) JavaScript engine QUnit suite. package prints brief startup message load, version pointers dagitty(), launch_dagittyplus(), web editor.
